commit 887ea38bfbf1c95259eb23dda7d4bd6923deae76
Author: Ethan Yang <ethan.yang.kernel@gmail.com>

LoongArch: mm: fix FLATMEM ARCH_PFN_OFFSET for non-zero DRAM base

This commit changes the definition of ARCH_PFN_OFFSET on LoongArch from
PFN_UP(PHYS_OFFSET) to min_low_pfn to handle non-zero DRAM bases. It also
updates the memory initialization paths to set min_low_pfn from the memblock
API.

> diff --git a/arch/loongarch/include/asm/page.h b/arch/loongarch/include/asm/page.h
> index 79235f4fc399..dac081ad991f 100644
> --- a/arch/loongarch/include/asm/page.h
> +++ b/arch/loongarch/include/asm/page.h
> @@ -25,7 +25,8 @@
>   * used in our early mem init code for all memory models.
>   * So always define it.
>   */
> -#define ARCH_PFN_OFFSET	PFN_UP(PHYS_OFFSET)
> +extern unsigned long min_low_pfn;
> +#define ARCH_PFN_OFFSET	min_low_pfn

Will this cause linker errors for loadable modules when CONFIG_FLATMEM is
enabled?

Because ARCH_PFN_OFFSET is used in inline functions like pfn_to_page() and
pfn_valid(), any loadable module calling these will directly reference
min_low_pfn. 

Since min_low_pfn is not exported via EXPORT_SYMBOL() in mm/memblock.c, it
seems this will result in an unknown symbol error during the modpost stage
for loadable modules.

[ ... ]

Target Commit:
commit 887ea38bfbf1c95259eb23dda7d4bd6923deae76
Author: Ethan Yang <ethan.yang.kernel@gmail.com>
Date:   Wed Apr 1 04:17:00 2026 +0000

    LoongArch: mm: fix FLATMEM ARCH_PFN_OFFSET for non-zero DRAM base
    
    With CONFIG_FLATMEM, pfn_to_page() is computed as:
    
      mem_map + (pfn - ARCH_PFN_OFFSET)
    
    LoongArch currently derives ARCH_PFN_OFFSET from PFN_UP(PHYS_OFFSET),
    while PHYS_OFFSET defaults to 0. On systems whose first DRAM range does
    not start at physical address 0, ARCH_PFN_OFFSET becomes smaller than
    the real RAM base PFN.
    
    As a result, pfn_to_page() points past the intended mem_map base during
    early memmap initialization. This causes struct page initialization to
    touch wrong entries and can lead to early boot failures in the
    memmap_init_range() path.
    
    Fix this by deriving ARCH_PFN_OFFSET from min_low_pfn, and initialize/
    recalculate min_low_pfn from memblock_start_of_DRAM() in LoongArch
    memory init so PFN-to-page translation matches the actual DRAM base.
    
    This change only fixes the FLATMEM PFN base and does not alter the
    existing VA/PA linear mapping scheme.
    
    Signed-off-by: Ethan Yang <ethan.yang.kernel@gmail.com>

diff --git a/arch/loongarch/include/asm/page.h b/arch/loongarch/include/asm/page.h
index 79235f4fc399..dac081ad991f 100644
--- a/arch/loongarch/include/asm/page.h
+++ b/arch/loongarch/include/asm/page.h
@@ -25,7 +25,8 @@
  * used in our early mem init code for all memory models.
  * So always define it.
  */
-#define ARCH_PFN_OFFSET	PFN_UP(PHYS_OFFSET)
+extern unsigned long min_low_pfn;
+#define ARCH_PFN_OFFSET	min_low_pfn
 
 extern void clear_page(void *page);
 extern void copy_page(void *to, void *from);
diff --git a/arch/loongarch/kernel/mem.c b/arch/loongarch/kernel/mem.c
index 8ab1ffedc52c..03835f8cb5fe 100644
--- a/arch/loongarch/kernel/mem.c
+++ b/arch/loongarch/kernel/mem.c
@@ -46,6 +46,7 @@ void __init memblock_init(void)
 		}
 	}
 
+	min_low_pfn = PFN_UP(memblock_start_of_DRAM());
 	max_pfn = PFN_DOWN(memblock_end_of_DRAM());
 	max_low_pfn = min(PFN_DOWN(HIGHMEM_START), max_pfn);
 	memblock_set_current_limit(PFN_PHYS(max_low_pfn));
diff --git a/arch/loongarch/kernel/setup.c b/arch/loongarch/kernel/setup.c
index 839b23edee87..625be2494f58 100644
--- a/arch/loongarch/kernel/setup.c
+++ b/arch/loongarch/kernel/setup.c
@@ -394,6 +394,7 @@ static void __init check_kernel_sections_mem(void)
 static void __init arch_mem_init(char **cmdline_p)
 {
 	/* Recalculate max_low_pfn for "mem=xxx" */
+	min_low_pfn = PFN_UP(memblock_start_of_DRAM());
 	max_pfn = PFN_DOWN(memblock_end_of_DRAM());
 	max_low_pfn = min(PFN_DOWN(HIGHMEM_START), max_pfn);

Okay, so I've confirmed something I suspected - `pfn_to_page` is a core function, *heavily* relied upon throughout the kernel, even within modules. That's a fundamental utility. I specifically looked at how it's defined within the `FLATMEM` memory model. It's essentially an offset calculation: `(mem_map + ((pfn) - ARCH_PFN_OFFSET))`. The key issue is that `ARCH_PFN_OFFSET` is directly tied to `min_low_pfn`. Now, any module out there trying to use `pfn_to_page` (or any macro that builds upon it, like `virt_to_page` or `page_to_virt`), will inherently need `min_low_pfn`.

Here's the rub: `min_low_pfn` *isn't* exported to modules in the LoongArch architecture. This means any module compiled for LoongArch that *does* depend on `CONFIG_FLATMEM` and subsequently uses `pfn_to_page` – which is highly likely given how fundamental it is – will simply fail to link. It’s a guaranteed linkage failure.

Now I need to take a step back and ask a fundamental question: Is `CONFIG_FLATMEM` even *allowed* on LoongArch? I need to check the architecture's Kconfig file, specifically `arch/loongarch/Kconfig`, to see if the option is enabled or if there are any conditional constraints. That's the next step, I need to see if this is even something the architecture supports at all.

Okay, let's break this down. My initial concern was about how `memblock_reserve` interacts with `memblock.memory`. Specifically, it reserves physical addresses, but it doesn't automatically add them to `memblock.memory`. This matters because the memory management subsystem *only* considers memory within `memblock.memory` as part of RAM. This immediately implies that the kernel *won't* create `struct page` entries for memory outside of that region. And if those `struct page` structures are absent, then `pfn_to_page` is going to fail for those physical addresses.

This realization is key. It means that `ARCH_PFN_OFFSET` *accurately* reflects the starting point for memory where `struct page`s are actually allocated, and therefore, `min_low_pfn` perfectly describes the lowest valid PFN for use with `pfn_to_page`. This all seems perfectly sound.

Importantly, for `SPARSEMEM`, `ARCH_PFN_OFFSET` isn't used for `pfn_to_page` translation anyway. No NUMA issues here!

If `ARCH_PFN_OFFSET` is now `min_low_pfn`, should `PHYS_OFFSET` also be changed to `PFN_PHYS(min_low_pfn)`? If `PHYS_OFFSET` is 0, as is typical, then `__va(x)` maps physical address 0 to `PAGE_OFFSET`. But then I remembered the author's explicit statement: "This change only fixes the FLATMEM PFN base and does not alter the existing VA/PA linear mapping scheme." If `PHYS_OFFSET` were changed, it *would* disrupt the direct mapping. Next, I looked at how `ARCH_PFN_OFFSET` is used, specifically in `early_memtest()`. The current logic tests memory from `ARCH_PFN_OFFSET` to `max_low_pfn`. The patch sets `ARCH_PFN_OFFSET` to `min_low_pfn`.  I see that before this, `ARCH_PFN_OFFSET` was `PFN_UP(PHYS_OFFSET)`, which was effectively zero. If the system's DRAM doesn't start at physical address zero, this was a *major* bug that could have resulted in testing nonexistent RAM and potentially crashing the system.  So, this fixes a critical error in how memory is tested during boot.

Now I considered the implications for the `__pa()` and `__va()` macros. These are important for physical-to-virtual address translations.  `__va(x)` assumes a linear mapping that starts at `PHYS_OFFSET`, which is *crucial* because if `PHYS_OFFSET` is zero,  `__va(x)` correctly maps a physical address `x` to `PAGE_OFFSET + x`.  I checked the LoongArch architecture and confirmed that `PAGE_OFFSET` is defined in `arch/loongarch/include/asm/page.h`, and, more importantly, LoongArch uses a direct mapping segment, like MIPS' `kseg0`, meaning `__va()` works by direct hardware mapping.  Therefore, having `PHYS_OFFSET` as zero is correct for `__pa()` and `__va()` because it leverages direct physical-to-virtual mapping. The author is correct in their assessment that this doesn't alter the existing VA/PA scheme.

My next concern was about the possibility of other code accidentally modifying `min_low_pfn` after initialization. Because it is a global variable, this would break the logic. However, I confirmed that after boot, there are no writes, so that eliminates one of my concerns.

Then, there was the export of `min_low_pfn`. If it's not exported, modules using `pfn_to_page()` or functions that rely on it would fail to link.  I started thinking about functions like `page_address()`, `kmap()`, and particularly `pfn_valid()` as these depend on it.

I then looked closely at `pfn_valid()`, it *does* use `ARCH_PFN_OFFSET`!  My immediate thought went to modules doing `pfn_valid()` and more specifically `virt_addr_valid()`.  On LoongArch, `__virt_addr_valid` is exported. Examining the code, `__virt_addr_valid` does the `pfn_valid` check *internally*. Therefore, external modules calling `virt_addr_valid` call the out-of-line `__virt_addr_valid` function, which *doesn't* require direct access to `pfn_valid` or `pfn_to_page`! This addresses one area of concern.

However, I started thinking about modules explicitly calling `pfn_valid()` or `pfn_to_page()`. There are several modules using `pfn_to_page()`, such as `sg_set_page()` and `vm_insert_page()`. `vm_insert_pfn` uses PFNs. So, it's quite likely there are modules that will need access to this value.  I must verify precisely which functions need it.

Okay, so I've been digging into the LoongArch memory configuration, and I've come across a significant issue. Specifically, if `!NUMA` is true on LoongArch, `ARCH_FLATMEM_ENABLE` is set to 'y', meaning `CONFIG_FLATMEM` *is* a valid option.

Here's the problem: `ARCH_PFN_OFFSET` plays a crucial role in `include/asm-generic/memory_model.h`, specifically in the definition of `__pfn_to_page`.  This function, in turn, underpins `pfn_to_page`.  So, any loadable module that tries to use `pfn_to_page` directly (or wraps it with something like `virt_to_page`) is going to fail to link because the linker will complain about an undefined symbol: `min_low_pfn`.

I need to confirm if `virt_to_page` actually utilizes `__pfn_to_page` in the LoongArch architecture, so I took a look in `arch/loongarch/include/asm/page.h`. I can see:
```c
#ifndef CONFIG_KFENCE

#define page_to_virt(page)	__va(page_to_phys(page))
#define virt_to_page(kaddr)	phys_to_page(__pa(kaddr))

#else
...
#endif
```

Now, `phys_to_page` is defined in `include/asm-generic/memory_model.h` as:

```c
#define phys_to_page(phys)	pfn_to_page(phys_to_pfn(phys))
```

And, yes, `pfn_to_page` is equivalent to `__pfn_to_page`.

So, there we have it. `virt_to_page`, which is used ALL OVER THE PLACE in drivers, uses `min_low_pfn`.  This means this change *will* absolutely break module building for LoongArch under a valid `CONFIG_FLATMEM` configuration.

This is a high-severity issue, breaking module compilation under a supported configuration, making it difficult to use.

The next step is to see if there's any mechanism, even a clever one, to export `min_low_pfn` in this environment.

    "thought": "**High-Severity LoongArch Module Loading Issue with CONFIG_FLATMEM**

Okay, so here's the deal. I've been digging into this LoongArch commit, and it looks like there's a serious problem with loadable modules when `CONFIG_FLATMEM` is enabled. Specifically, the commit redefines `ARCH_PFN_OFFSET` as `min_low_pfn`, which, in itself, isn't necessarily wrong, but the consequences are.

The issue stems from how `__pfn_to_page` (and related macros like `__page_to_pfn` and `pfn_valid`) are defined as inline macros within `include/asm-generic/memory_model.h` when `CONFIG_FLATMEM` is active. This means that whenever these macros are used, they're expanded directly in the code. And guess what? These macros ultimately reference `min_low_pfn`. The killer is that `min_low_pfn` is defined in `mm/memblock.c` without being explicitly exported using `EXPORT_SYMBOL`.

The implication is pretty clear. Any loadable module (and, let's be honest, *a lot* of them use `pfn_to_page()`, `virt_to_page()`, or `pfn_valid()` which, in turn, use the problematic macro expansions) will have an unresolved symbol: `min_low_pfn`. This lack of visibility during the link stage is a showstopper. The `modpost` process will flag this as an "Unknown symbol" error, and the module simply won't load. The architecture has effectively broken module support under `CONFIG_FLATMEM`!

Given that `CONFIG_FLATMEM` is selectable on LoongArch by default when `!NUMA`, this is a pretty critical flaw. And `virt_to_page()`, for example, expands through `phys_to_page()`, `pfn_to_page()` to hit `__pfn_to_page()`. So there is no way around it.

To fix this, LoongArch needs to do one of two things: either utilize an architecture-specific exported symbol for `ARCH_PFN_OFFSET` instead of `min_low_pfn`, or, and I'd recommend this as the easier fix, explicitly export `min_low_pfn` with `EXPORT_SYMBOL`.

This is a High-severity issue because it completely breaks module loading under a common configuration (`CONFIG_FLATMEM`) and will require a fix to avoid a very obvious regression.
